key_insight: Gradient detonation-sprayed NiCr-Al coatings outperform homogeneous coatings in high-temperature oxidation by maintaining a more protective, stable oxide layer and reducing degradation.
engineering_relevance: For thermal power plant components, tailoring coating composition through a gradient architecture can extend service life under severe heat and corrosive operating conditions.
method: Detonation-sprayed NiCr-Al coatings were prepared in both gradient and homogeneous architectures.
Samples were tested under real thermal power plant operating conditions at high temperature.
Degradation was analyzed using phase composition checks and SEM microstructural examination, including oxide-layer evolution and elemental redistribution.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that gradient coatings retained structural integrity better, showed more uniform Al/Cr distribution, and formed continuous Al2O3/Cr2O3 scales, while homogeneous coatings suffered Al depletion, cracking, and spallation.
watch_item: Performance is demonstrated under one operating environment, so durability and scalability should still be validated across longer exposures, varying temperatures, and component geometries.
paper_title: Investigation of Detonation-Sprayed NiCr–Al Coatings Tested under Real Operating Conditions of a Thermal Power Plant
